title: How chronic tension patterns map to specific traumas

htmlContent:
Your body is a map. And it's telling a story you might not want to read.

I've been thinking a lot about this thing that happens to us. The way we hold ourselves. The way our shoulders creep up toward our ears when we're stressed. The way our jaw clenches at night. The way our lower back locks up after a hard week.

We call it tension. We call it stress. We blame our posture, our desk chairs, our mattresses. We stretch, we foam roll, we get massages. And the tension comes back. Every time. Because here's the thing: chronic tension patterns aren't just physical. They're a memory. A memory your body never got to finish processing.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, spent decades watching how animals in the wild shake off life-threatening experiences. They don't develop chronic tension. They don't hold patterns. They complete the cycle. But we humans? We freeze. We brace. We lock. And that locking becomes a permanent resident in our tissues.

I want to walk you through what I've learned about how specific tension patterns map to specific traumas. Not as some neat taxonomy. Not as a diagnosis. But as a way to start understanding what your body has been saying all these years. Stay with me here.

The shoulders and the weight of "I can't"

Let's start with something almost everyone knows: tight shoulders. Rounded forward. Hunched. Like you're bracing for a blow that never comes.

Think about what that posture says. It says "I'm protecting my chest. I'm making myself smaller. I'm ready to defend." And that's exactly what it is. Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote The Body Keeps the Score (paid link), showed us that trauma isn't just a story in your head. It's a physiological response stored in your body's tissues. Your shoulders remember the moment you had to brace for impact. Or the moment you had to shrink to survive.

I see this pattern most in people who grew up in unpredictable environments. Homes where you never knew when the next explosion would come. Where you learned to make yourself small, to not take up space, to prepare for the worst. Your shoulders carry that preparation. They never got the signal that it's safe to let go.

Here's what's wild: you can stretch those shoulders every day. You can get deep tissue work. You can roll them out on a TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link) until you cry from the pain. And they'll tighten right back up. Because the tension isn't a muscle problem. It's a safety problem. Your body still believes it needs that armor.

The fix isn't just physical. It's nervous system work. It's teaching your body that you're safe now. That you don't need to brace. That the danger passed. And that takes time. It takes patience. It takes a kind of listening most of us were never taught.

The jaw, the throat, and the words you swallowed

Let's talk about the jaw. That tight, aching, teeth-grinding, TMJ-destroying tension that so many of us carry.

Think about what the jaw does. It bites. It speaks. It holds back. When you clench your jaw, you're doing something very specific: you're stopping something from coming out. You're holding words in. You're biting down on a scream that never got to leave your body.

I've seen this pattern most in people who were silenced. People who were told to be quiet. People who learned that speaking up was dangerous. People who swallowed their anger, their protest, their "no" because saying it would have gotten them hurt. That swallowing becomes a permanent pattern. Your jaw locks. Your throat tightens. Your voice gets small.

Janina Fisher, who integrates trauma treatment with parts work, talks about how different parts of us hold different responses. There's the part that wanted to scream. There's the part that learned to clamp down. There's the part that still believes silence equals safety. All of them live in your jaw, your throat, your vocal cords.

And here's the tricky part. Releasing that tension isn't just about relaxing your jaw. It's about letting those swallowed words exist. It's about finding a safe way to let that sound out. Even if it's just in your car. Even if it's just on a pillow. Even if it's just a whispered "no" to yourself in the mirror. Your body needs to know it's allowed to speak.

The pelvis, the hips, and the freeze response

This one is harder to talk about. Because the pelvis holds so much. Sexuality. Power. Safety. Grounding. And for many trauma survivors, it holds the freeze.

Think about the freeze response. When fight or flight aren't options, when you can't run and you can't fight, your body does the only thing it can. It goes still. It plays dead. It numbs out. And that freeze gets stored in the deepest tissues of your body. In your psoas. In your hips. In your pelvic floor.

I notice this pattern in people who experienced boundary violations. People who had their "no" ignored. People who learned that resistance was futile. Their hips lock up. Their pelvis tightens. Their legs feel disconnected from their torso. It's like the body is still trying to stay still, still trying to survive by not moving.

Peter Levine talks about how the key to releasing trauma is completing the incomplete responses. Your body wanted to run. It wanted to kick. It wanted to push away. But it couldn't. So those impulses got stuck. They live in your hips as chronic tension.

This is where somatic work gets really interesting. Because you don't have to relive the trauma. You don't have to tell the story. You just have to let your body know it can move now. It can push. It can kick. It can run. Even if it's just tiny movements. Even if it's just the impulse to move. Your body needs to complete what got interrupted.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link). Levine walks through this process in detail. It's not an easy read. But it's a real one.

The chest and the armor around your heart

Let's talk about the chest. That tightness around your heart. That shallow breathing. That feeling like you can't take a full breath.

This pattern maps to attachment wounds. To loss. To grief that never got to move through you. To the moment you learned that love was dangerous, that connection meant pain, that opening your heart would get you hurt.

Your chest tightens to protect your heart. Literally. The muscles around your ribcage contract. Your diaphragm gets tight. Your breath becomes shallow. You're creating a physical barrier between your heart and the world. It's brilliant, really. Your body found a way to protect you from the deepest pain. But it also cut you off from the deepest joy.

Dan Siegel, who studies interpersonal neurobiology, talks about how our relationships shape our brains and our bodies. When early relationships are unsafe, our nervous system learns to protect. That protection lives in your chest. In your breath. In the way you hold yourself back from fully feeling.

I see this pattern in people who were abandoned. People who lost someone too young. People who were betrayed by someone they trusted. Their chest holds the memory of that loss. It's bracing against the next one.

Releasing this pattern is slow work. It's about learning to breathe fully again. It's about letting your heart be soft, even when it's terrified. It's about small moments of safety, repeated over and over, until your body starts to believe that maybe, just maybe, it's okay to open.

A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is the YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). The weight can help your body feel held, feel safe, feel grounded. It's not a cure. But it's a comfort. And comfort matters.

What do we do with this map?

I'm not going to tell you there's a simple fix. There isn't. Chronic tension patterns took years to develop. They'll take time to release. And they might never fully go away. That's okay.

What I want you to take from this is curiosity. When you notice that knot in your shoulder, instead of just trying to stretch it out, ask it a question. What are you protecting? What memory are you holding? What response never got to complete?

Your body isn't broken. It's not betraying you. It's doing exactly what it learned to do to keep you alive. And that's worthy of respect, not frustration.

The work isn't about forcing your body to let go. It's about building enough safety that your body chooses to let go on its own. It's about listening. It's about patience. It's about yourself, even when it's uncomfortable.

Start small. Notice one pattern. Breathe into it. Let yourself feel the impulse that lives underneath. Maybe it wants to move. Maybe it wants to make a sound. Maybe it just wants to be acknowledged. Whatever it is, give it space. Give it time.

Your body has been talking to you your whole life. It's time to start listening.
